name: Earth Oven Roast Pig
recipeInstructions:
1. The night before, have some strong men dig a pit 10-15 inches deep.
2. Find small (1 to 2 lb) lava (porous) rock and some hard wood (e.g., Eucalyptus, Oak).
3. Layer the wood and rock to make a large mound in the pit and set it on fire around 3 AM.
4. By 7 AM, coals and white-hot rocks will be ready.
5. Check each hour for readiness.
6. Salt and pepper the pig inside and out thoroughly; let it come to room temperature.
7. Cover the rock/embers with 3 to 6 layers of wet banana leaves (or seaweed, corn stalks, or similar).
8. Cover this with layers of wet burlap sacks, starting from the outside and layering like shingles to the middle.
9. Place the pit on a small section of a wire fence (like for chicks).
10. Using tongs, put several hot rocks in the pig cavity near the thickest part of the meat.
11. Spread one layer banana leaves on burlap and place the pig in the middle of the pit (encased in the wire).
12. Cover the pig with more banana leaves, then cover again with burlap, starting from the outside and layering like shingles to the middle.
13. Shovel dirt back into the pit completely covering it; do not leave any holes uncovered.
14. Lightly sprinkle with a garden hose to allow steam to escape but keep well covered.
15. About 4 or 5 in the afternoon, the roast should be done. It is also fun to add chickens, whole fish, or goats wrapped tightly in foil.
16. Carefully shovel dirt from the top of the pit.
17. When the burlap in the middle is seen, start rolling it back toward the sides and ends.
18. Serve the fully cooked pig on a picnic table for guests to pick their own pieces.
19. Serve with Hawaiian poi and sea salt; treat poi like bread and never eat it alone but always with hunks of pork.
